cluster_recommendations.issue: Cluster-wide citation vacuum — no named research backing on any article
cluster_recommendations.fix: Add a minimum of 3 named, attributed citations per article using the format 'According to [Author, Year].' Prioritize: Vygotsky (ZPD foundational theory, 1978), Wood, Bruner & Ross (scaffolding original framework, 1976), Hattie (Visible Learning, 2009 — effect sizes for feedback and scaffolding), and recent RAND or TNTP practitioner studies. Replace every instance of 'research shows' with a named citation. This single change will lift E-E-A-T Authoritativeness from ~30 to ~65 and unlock AI citation eligibility.

cluster_recommendations.issue: Zero quantitative data across the entire cluster
cluster_recommendations.fix: Inject at least 2 named statistics per article with source and year. Examples: Hattie's effect size for scaffolding (d=0.53, Visible Learning 2009), NWEA 2023 MAP data on learning recovery gaps, or specific classroom outcome percentages from published district studies. Format: '[X]% of students [outcome], according to [Source, Year].' This will lift Statistical Density sub-scores from ~20/100 to ~55–65/100 and directly improve AI Citability composites.

cluster_recommendations.fix: Add a dedicated 'Frequently asked questions' section (sentence case, H2) to each article containing 4–5 Q&A pairs. Use H3 question headings. Each answer: 40–60 words, self-contained, directly answerable without surrounding context. Sample questions: 'What is scaffolding in education?', 'What are the main types of scaffolding?', 'How is scaffolding different from differentiation?', 'When should teachers remove scaffolds?'. This enables FAQPage schema (already recommended in schema scores for all three articles) and activates Perplexity citation eligibility.

cluster_recommendations.issue: Hypothetical-only practitioner scenarios — no authentic classroom voices
cluster_recommendations.fix: Replace at least one illustrative vignette per article with an attributed real-world example. Options: pull from Wayground's teacher vetting network (~30 educators in review loop), use first-person UXR signals ('In a survey of X Wayground teachers...'), or cite published case studies from ASCD, Edutopia, or district reports. Even one real attribution ('Ms. [Name], 4th-grade teacher, [School/District]') signals E-E-A-T Experience to both Google Quality Raters and AI systems.

cluster_recommendations.fix: Item 73 ('integrated scaffolding concepts') scored 24/100 on Brand Voice and is the only confirmed FAIL in the cluster. Prioritize this article for brand remediation before investing in GEO improvements — low brand score (24) applies a 15% weight drag that suppresses GEO Composite regardless of citation or structure improvements. Address naming conventions, em dash usage, heading case, and positioning language first.

# Cluster Analysis: Scaffolding / Core (Items 71–73)

## Cluster overview

The Scaffolding / Core cluster is publishing below threshold across all three articles, with an average GEO Composite of **53.8/100** and a QAPEDS average of **5.6** (Weak tier). The cluster's structural scaffolding (ironic, given the topic) is in reasonable shape — schema recommendations are strong and two of three articles carry correct heading hierarchies — but the content layer is critically underfunded. Every article in this cluster reads like a knowledgeable writer summarizing a topic rather than an authoritative source making verifiable claims.

---

## Score summary table

| Dimension | Cluster Avg | Target (70+) | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI Citability | 55.7 | 70 | -14.3 |
| E-E-A-T | **48.0** | 70 | **-22.0** ← weakest |
| Platform Optimization | 48.3 | 70 | -21.7 |
| Brand Voice | n/a (pending) | 70 | — |
| SEO Structure | 58.0 | 70 | -12.0 |
| Schema Recommendations | **79.0** | 70 | +9.0 ← strongest |
| **GEO Composite** | **53.8** | 70 | **-16.2** |
| **QAPEDS** | **5.6/10** | 8.0 | **-2.4** |

Schema is the one bright spot — all three articles already have strong schema recommendations in place, meaning the structural scaffolding for AI discoverability exists. The problem is that the content sitting inside that structure has nothing for AI systems to cite.

---

## Top 3 recurring issues

### 1. No named research citations — affects all 3 articles (E-E-A-T, QAPEDS P-score)

Every article in this cluster uses generic attribution language — "research shows," "studies suggest," "experts recommend" — without naming a single author, institution, or year. Under QAPEDS rules, this caps Evidence Presence at ≤1 regardless of how much supporting content surrounds it. Under E-E-A-T, Authoritativeness scores are stuck at 30–40/100 cluster-wide. This is the single highest-leverage fix available: adding 3 named citations per article costs minimal editorial effort and unlocks AI citation eligibility across Google AIO, ChatGPT, and Gemini simultaneously.

### 2. Zero quantitative data — affects all 3 articles (AI Citability Statistical Density)

Statistical Density scored approximately 20/100 on every article in this cluster — the lowest possible signal band. There are no percentages, no effect sizes, no sample sizes, no year-stamped outcome data anywhere across the three pieces. AI systems extract and re-cite statistics more than any other content type (GEO research: +33% citation lift from statistics). A cluster about scaffolding — a topic with rich quantitative backing from Hattie's Visible Learning meta-analyses, NWEA learning gap data, and RAND instructional practice studies — has no excuse for this gap.

### 3. Hypothetical-only classroom scenarios — affects all 3 articles (E-E-A-T Experience)

Every practitioner example in the cluster is either an invented vignette (named "Maria" or similarly generic) or a non-attributed hypothetical. Google Quality Raters and AI systems differentiate between illustrative fiction and actual practitioner experience. The Experience sub-score sits at 35–50/100 across the cluster. Wayground has a teacher vetting network of approximately 30 educators — even one attributed real-world example per article would meaningfully shift these scores.

---

## Platform readiness

| Platform | Ready | % |
|---|---|---|
| AIO (Google) | 2 of 3 | 67% |
| ChatGPT | 1 of 3 | 33% |
| Perplexity | **0 of 3** | **0%** |

AIO readiness is the cluster's relative strength — two articles have direct answer blocks and structured headings sufficient for Google's AI Overviews. The ChatGPT gap (only item 72 qualifies) reflects the missing factual density: ChatGPT requires content that directly answers its title question with verifiable data, and most articles trail off into narrative before delivering the goods.

Perplexity readiness at **0%** is the most urgent platform gap. Perplexity is highly FAQ-dependent, and none of the three articles contain a structured FAQ block. The schema recommendations already call for FAQPage schema on all three articles — but that schema cannot activate without actual FAQ content in the HTML. This is a one-to-one fix: add FAQ sections, get Perplexity-ready.

---

## Brand compliance

| Verdict | Count |
|---|---|
| PASS | 0 |
| WARN | 0 |
| FAIL | 1 (item 73) |
| UNKNOWN (pending review) | 2 (items 71, 72) |

Item 73 ("integrated scaffolding concepts") is a confirmed brand FAIL at 24/100 — the lowest brand score in the cluster and low enough to suppress GEO Composite by roughly 7 points through the 15% weighting. Two articles are still pending brand review. This means the cluster has **zero confirmed brand-clean articles** at time of analysis. Brand remediation on item 73 is a prerequisite, not a parallel track — GEO investments made before fixing brand compliance will be partially negated.

---

## Cluster-level recommendations

**1. Inject named citations on every article (all 3 articles | E-E-A-T + AI Citability)**
Replace every "research shows" / "studies suggest" with attributed sources. Start with foundational scaffolding literature: Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development (1978), Wood, Bruner & Ross's original scaffolding framework (1976), and Hattie's Visible Learning (2009) effect sizes. Add at least one contemporary source per article (post-2020) — RAND, NWEA, or TNTP practitioner research qualify. Minimum: 3 named citations per article, formatted as "According to [Author, Year]."

**2. Add quantitative data points to every article (all 3 articles | AI Citability)**
Target 2 named statistics per article with source and year. Hattie's scaffolding effect size (d=0.53) is the most defensible anchor stat for this cluster. Layer in NWEA 2023 MAP data for grade-level gap context, or specific outcome percentages from published district implementations. Format every stat as "[X]% of [population] [outcome], according to [Source, Year]" to maximize AI extractability.

**3. Add FAQ sections to activate Perplexity readiness (all 3 articles | Platform Optimization)**
Schema recommendations already call for FAQPage schema — the content just needs to catch up. Add a "Frequently asked questions" H2 section to each article with 4–5 Q&A pairs. H3 heading = the question. Answer paragraph = 40–60 words, self-contained. Questions should mirror real search queries: "What is scaffolding in education?", "What are the types of scaffolding?", "How does scaffolding differ from differentiation?", "When should you remove a scaffold?" This single structural addition lifts Perplexity readiness from 0% to a potential 67–100%.

**4. Ground at least one classroom scenario per article in real attribution (all 3 articles | E-E-A-T)**
Draw from Wayground's teacher vetting network, published ASCD or Edutopia case studies, or first-party UXR data ("In feedback from X Wayground teachers..."). A named teacher with a grade level and school context — even without a surname — signals authentic practitioner experience to both Quality Raters and AI systems in a way that "Ms. Smith's fourth-grade class" cannot.

**5. Prioritize brand remediation on item 73 before other edits (item 73 | Brand Voice)**
At 24/100 brand voice, item 73 is carrying a structural penalty that will offset gains in every other dimension. Fix naming conventions, eliminate em dashes from body copy, correct heading case, and verify positioning language before investing editorial hours in citation or FAQ additions.

---

## Standout articles

**Strongest: Item 72 — "Types of scaffolding in education" (GEO: 58.0)**
The only ChatGPT-ready article in the cluster and the highest QAPEDS score (6.5). It has the best AI Citability score (62) and the most coherent content-answer fit. Still critically weak on E-E-A-T and statistical density, but structurally the most repair-ready article. Prioritize this one for citation and FAQ additions — it will respond fastest to targeted fixes.

**Weakest: Item 73 — "Integrated scaffolding concepts" (GEO: 46.5)**
The only article with zero platform readiness across all five platforms and the only confirmed brand FAIL. QAPEDS at 4.55 (Weak) reflects both the missing direct answer block and the evidence vacuum. This article requires the most comprehensive intervention: brand remediation first, then structural re-architecture
